headline: From Waste to Wisdom: How Menstrual Blood Became Medicine
meta_description: A frustrated woman's question sparked a revolution: could menstrual blood transform women's health diagnostics forever?
summary_short: When a gynecologist refused to order a fertility test, Ridhi Tariyal decided to reimagine the biological material women discard monthly into a powerful diagnostic tool.
body:
When Ridhi Tariyal's doctor refused to order a fertility test, dismissing it as impossible, she knew better. With degrees from Harvard and MIT, she understood the science existed—her doctor simply wouldn't provide it.

That frustration became fuel for innovation. Tariyal realized that the biological material women discard monthly contains over 800 genes expressed differently than in venous blood. "We throw all this valuable information into the trash," she says.

In 2014, together with biologist Stephen Gire, Tariyal founded NextGen Jane. The company developed patented methods for collecting blood samples from tampons. Women now seal used tampons in specially designed kits and mail them to the Oakland, California laboratory. A machine smaller than an espresso machine extracts viable samples in seconds—without speculums, stirrups, or invasive procedures.

The results are striking. NextGen Jane identified a biomarker panel for endometriosis with over 90 percent accuracy. For the one in ten women of reproductive age affected by this painful condition, diagnosis typically takes seven to ten years and requires surgery under general anesthesia.

What began as one woman's frustration has grown into a thriving field. Stanford researchers now use menstrual blood to detect cancer-causing HPV strains. Abbott released a tampon PCR test for endometrial cancer. The ROSE trial at Northwell Health developed a nonsurgical endometriosis test using menstrual cups.

The innovation reveals an overlooked truth: sometimes the most valuable solutions hide in plain sight, waiting for someone frustrated enough to ask the right question.
